comments.author: F2Scout
comments.text: Thompson’s season so far is absolutely wild. In F2 he’s a ghost — 16th in Melbourne, never looked like scoring. Then he climbs into a GT3 Aston with no factory support, at the only oval on the calendar, and wins both races. That’s 300 points from a single weekend out of nowhere. If he could bring even a fraction of this oval pace to a road course, Mercedes might actually have a prospect worth the investment.

replies.author: PaddockInsider
replies.text: The stats don’t lie: without Auto Club, Thompson would have zero podiums and a handful of backmarker finishes in GT3. The 602 points total flatters him — it’s literally just those two wins plus some scraps from Indy. He’s either a genius on ovals or a passenger everywhere else. No in-between.

comments.author: TridentFan
comments.text: As a Trident fan, seeing this is painful. Our car is clearly quick — Grunt took pole and won the F2 feature in Melbourne. Thompson’s teammate Vavro set the fastest lap before crashing. So why is Thompson P16 while driving the same chassis? He’s clearly got talent, but it’s locked behind some mental block on road courses. I hope he finds the key before Bahrain.

replies.author: RealityCheck
replies.text: Vavro’s fastest lap was on a drying track and he still binned it. Thompson at least kept it clean in the feature race until that late spin. I’d argue consistency is the bigger issue for both of them. Trident have the pace, but these two are chaotic.

comments.author: MercedesJuniorWatch
comments.text: Why is a Mercedes-backed F2 driver racing an Aston Martin GT3 with no Silver Arrows stickers? The series has a fleet of AMG GT3s — surely Mercedes could have placed him in one. Doing it as a privateer is a weird flex, but maybe it’s a sign that Mercedes are keeping him at arm’s length. Two wins are great, but they don’t hide the DNFs and 40th places on road courses.

replies.author: F2Scout
replies.text: Thompson basically said it himself: he’s just a guy in a green car trying to prove he can win anywhere. That’s either self-confidence or a subtle dig at Mercedes for not giving him a full factory GT ride. Either way, winning in a customer Aston against a field stacked with factory-backed cars is legitimately impressive, even if it’s only happened once.

comments.author: DNF_Fakt
comments.text: Thompson’s quote about the zeros hurting but the wins making him feel like he belongs — that’s the most relatable thing I’ve ever read. It’s also the exact emotional rollercoaster of being a Sam Fakt fan, except Thompson has actually won something this year. Maybe there’s hope for our boy yet.

replies.author: FaktLap1Legend
replies.text: Thompson winning a pair of GT3 races in a borrowed Aston is cute. But the GOAT Sam Fakt doesn’t need to prove anything in a minor series. He’s saving his wins for the big stage — like that future F0 championship you keep forgetting exists. 🐐

replies.author: DNF_Fakt
replies.text: I haven’t forgotten. I’ve just chosen to live in reality. Thompson has two wins. Fakt has two lap-one DNFs. The GOAT needs to actually finish a lap before we talk about imaginary championships.

comments.author: GridGazer
comments.text: The Aston Martin team principal calling the oval a ‘freak result’ is telling. Even his own team doesn’t fully understand why he was so dominant there. The next oval at Jacarepaguá later in the season will be the real test — if he sweeps that too, he’s an oval specialist and someone in IndyCar should be paying attention. If he bins it, then Auto Club was a beautiful one-off.

comments.author: ChaosReigns
comments.text: Only in 2026 can a Mercedes junior driver win two races in a championship full of Mercedes cars while driving a non-Mercedes, then fly back to F2 and be anonymous. Thompson’s season is the definition of “you can’t script this.” I’ll be watching Curvelo to see if he’s finally cracked the road course curse, but I’m not betting the house on it.
